explanation: The requirement states that if both prop_2 and prop_3 hold, then exceeding sensor limits (represented as prop_1) shall also hold. In LTL, this is a safety property: globally, if prop_2 and prop_3 are true, then prop_1 must also be true at that time.

The requirement states that 'Exceeding sensor limits shall prop_1 when prop_2 (prop_3) and prop_4 (prop_5)'. This implies that if both (prop_2 or prop_3) and (prop_4 or prop_5) hold, then prop_1 must hold as a consequence. In LTL, this is usually expressed as globally: if (prop_2 or prop_3) and (prop_4 or prop_5) then prop_1.

The requirement means that whenever prop_2 and prop_3 hold, prop_0 must hold and prop_1 must hold at the same time. That is, globally, if both prop_2 and prop_3 are true, then prop_0 and prop_1 must also be true at that time.

The specification requires that 'Exceeding sensor limits' (assumed as prop_1) must occur when both prop_2 (with prop_3) and prop_4 (with prop_5) are true. This is interpreted as: whenever (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) holds, prop_1 must also hold at that time. In LTL, this is most directly expressed as 'Globally, (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) -> prop_1'.

The requirement expresses that if both prop_2 (with prop_3) and prop_4 (with prop_5) hold, then prop_1 (exceeding sensor limits) must hold as well. In LTL, this can be reflected by stating that it is always the case (G) that whenever (->) (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) holds, prop_1 holds.

The statement requires that whenever both prop_2 is true and prop_3 is false, AND prop_4 is true and prop_5 is false (that is, at a time when both (prop_2 & !prop_3) and (prop_4 & !prop_5) are true), then it must hold that prop_0 implies prop_1 at that point in time. Therefore, globally, if all those conditions are true, then prop_0 -> prop_1.

The requirement says that when 'prop_2' and 'prop_3' are true, and 'prop_4' is true, and there are no failures ('not prop_5'), then 'prop_1' shall occur (hold). The LTL formula encodes that globally, whenever all these conditions hold together, 'prop_1' must also hold at the same time.

The requirement says that exceeding sensor limits (which we map to an obligation 'shall prop_1') should happen when both (prop_2 and prop_3) and (prop_4 and prop_5) are true. In LTL, we can express that when all these are true, then prop_1 must hold. This is ideally written as: (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & prop_5) -> prop_1. Assuming this must hold at all times, we wrap it with the globally (G) operator.

When both prop_2 and prop_3 are true, and both prop_4 and NOT prop_5 are true (i.e., 'without failures'), then prop_0 shall latch prop_1. In LTL, 'latch' is often interpreted as: once the condition is met, prop_0 keeps prop_1 true from that point on (prop_1 holds globally after the event). The trigger condition is (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & !prop_5), and upon this becoming true, prop_1 must hold globally afterwards. This is expressed as: whenever the condition occurs, (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4 & !prop_5) -> G(prop_1).

The requirement states that whenever prop_2 and prop_3 hold, and prop_4 holds while prop_5 (failures) does not hold, then prop_1 must occur (possibly in response to exceeding sensor limits). This translates to: globally, if (prop_2 and prop_3) and (prop_4 and not prop_5), then prop_1.
